title: CSR: Check-In, Check-Out & Front Desk Flow
description: The operational backbone of the front desk: smooth check-ins, accurate check-outs, managing the lobby, coordinating with the medical team, and keeping information and patients moving correctly.

blocks.content:
Check-in sets up the entire visit for success. A good check-in:
• Greets the client warmly by name when possible.
• Confirms the reason for the visit and updates any changed info (address, phone, etc.).
• Verifies the patient's details.
• Flags anything the medical team needs to know (an aggressive note, a fearful patient, a new concern the owner mentions).
• Sets expectations: 'The technician will be right with you.'

The information you gather and pass back determines whether the medical team is prepared. A check-in that captures 'owner says limping started after a fall yesterday' gives the team a head start.

Listen for new information at check-in. Owners often mention the real concern casually — 'oh, and he's been drinking a ton of water' — that the appointment wasn't booked for. Capture it and pass it back.

blocks.front: Why is information captured at check-in so important?
blocks.back: It determines whether the medical team is prepared — new details the owner mentions can change the whole visit.

blocks.question: At check-in, an owner casually mentions their cat 'has been throwing up a lot lately,' though the visit was booked for a nail trim. What do you do?

options.text: Ignore it since the visit is just for nails
options.feedback: New symptoms matter — the medical team should know.

options.text: Note it and pass it to the medical team so they can address it
options.feedback: Correct — capture the new concern and relay it; it may change the visit.
Never diagnose — just capture and relay.

blocks.content:
Check-out is where the visit's loose ends get tied — and where errors cause problems later.

A solid check-out:
• Reviews what the doctor recommended (follow-up visits, medications, instructions).
• Goes over the invoice clearly, matching it to the estimate.
• Schedules any follow-ups or rechecks before they leave.
• Ensures the client leaves with medications, written instructions, and a clear understanding of next steps.
• Confirms they have no lingering questions.

The goal: the client walks out knowing exactly what happens next and why. A confused client becomes a phone call (or a missed follow-up) later.
Book the follow-up before they leave, not 'call us later.' Appointments scheduled at checkout actually happen; 'call us later' often doesn't.

blocks.front: Why schedule follow-ups during check-out rather than telling the client to call later?
blocks.back: Follow-ups booked at checkout actually happen; 'call us later' often falls through.

blocks.prompt: At check-out, the invoice is noticeably higher than the estimate the client signed. What's the right approach?

options.text: Process it quietly and hope they don't notice
options.feedback: This guarantees an angry call later and destroys trust.

options.text: Proactively explain the difference, check it's correct, and involve the medical team or a manager if needed
options.feedback: Correct — transparency about the difference prevents conflict and shows integrity.

options.text: Tell them estimates never mean anything
options.feedback: Dismissive and untrue; estimates matter and discrepancies deserve explanation.

blocks.content:
The CSR runs the front-of-house like a conductor — managing the physical lobby and the flow of information between clients and the medical team.

Lobby management:
• Keep an eye on the waiting area; separate anxious or reactive animals when possible (a fearful cat next to a barking dog is a problem).
• Watch for animals or people in distress.
• Keep the space calm and welcoming.

Team coordination:
• You're the bridge between the back and the front. Relay information accurately in both directions.
• Keep the medical team aware of arrivals, walk-ins, and waiting clients.
• Communicate delays and changes promptly.

A great CSR makes the whole clinic run more smoothly because information flows correctly through them.
In the lobby, watch animal body language. Separating a terrified cat carrier from an excited dog before there's an incident is the kind of quiet save that prevents chaos.

blocks.front: How is the CSR like a conductor?
blocks.back: They manage the lobby and the flow of information between clients and the medical team, keeping the whole clinic running smoothly.

blocks.content: You've mastered the front-desk backbone: smooth check-ins that prepare the team, accurate check-outs that prevent problems, and lobby/team coordination that keeps everything flowing. You're the operational heart of the clinic.
